title: Iran Ammonia Supply Shock
catalyst: The Iran conflict has wiped out approximately 30% of global ammonia production capacity. With ammonia being a critical feedstock for nitrogen fertilizers, this supply shock creates an immediate pricing tailwind for surviving producers with existing capacity and distribution networks.
thesis: Global ammonia supply has contracted sharply due to the destruction of Iranian production facilities. Fertilizer prices are spiking as the spring planting season approaches in the Northern Hemisphere. Companies with diversified ammonia and nitrogen production outside the conflict zone stand to benefit from both higher prices and increased market share as buyers scramble for alternative supply.

ticker_rationale.NTR: Nutrien is the world's largest crop inputs company with significant ammonia and nitrogen production capacity in Canada, far from the conflict zone. With existing infrastructure and distribution networks across North America, Nutrien is positioned to capture displaced demand and benefit from elevated fertilizer pricing.

risks: Ceasefire or rapid reconstruction could normalize ammonia supply faster than expected
Government price controls on fertilizers could cap upside
Elevated input costs (natural gas) may compress margins despite higher fertilizer prices
sources: Reuters — Iran conflict impact on global ammonia production, March 2026
USDA — Spring planting season fertilizer demand forecast, 2026
